quotes.text: Penford
the Company
board of directors
Executive Committee
Ingredion
SEACOR
Party A
Party B
Party C
Party D
Party E
Party F
Party G
Deutsche Bank as the Company’s financial advisor
Perkins Coie LLP (referred to as Perkins Coie), Penford’s legal counsel
J. P. Morgan Securities, Ingredion’s financial advisor
Sidley Austin LLP (referred to as Sidley Austin), Ingredion’s legal counsel
Milbank, Tweed, Hadley & McCloy, LLP (referred to as Milbank), legal counsel to SEACOR

On July 11, 2014, SEACOR filed an amendment to its Schedule 13D (referred to as the SEACOR Filing) with the Securities and Exchange Commission (referred to as the SEC) in which SEACOR stated its intent to nominate four candidates for election as directors of our board at Penford’s next annual meeting of shareholders.
On July 17, 2014, Mr. Malkoski and Ms. Gordon met for lunch, during which meeting Ms. Gordon advised Mr. Malkoski of Ingredion’s interest in acquiring Penford.

Subsequently, Penford and Ingredion executed a Secrecy Agreement, effective as of July 20, 2014, providing for limited exchange of confidential information and obligating the companies to keep their discussions confidential in order to facilitate the providing of confidential information of Penford to Ingredion.

Mr. Hatfield recommended that the Executive Committee consider retaining a financial advisor on the Company’s behalf in light of these developments, and management reviewed the credentials of several firms and reported on interview meetings management had with certain firms regarding a potential engagement. Following discussion of potential financial advisors, the Executive Committee directed management to proceed to retain Deutsche Bank as the Company’s financial advisor and to advise on shareholder matters and to evaluate strategic alternatives, and directed management to negotiate a suitable engagement letter.
An engagement letter with Deutsche Bank was executed on September 11, 2014.

On August 6, 2014, Mr. Malkoski met with Mr. Fortnum, at which meeting Mr. Fortnum indicated Ingredion was prepared to submit a proposal to acquire the Company. Mr. Fortnum suggested that a price of $17.00 per share on a fully diluted basis could likely get support from Ingredion’s board.

On August 10, 2014, Penford received a letter from Mr. Fortnum, confirming Ingredion’s interest in a possible business combination between Ingredion and the Company, citing certain strategic reasons for the proposed acquisition, and providing an indicative valuation of $18.00 per share in cash for all of the outstanding capital stock of Penford on a fully diluted basis.
Deutsche Bank noted that while Ingredion’s proposal was non-binding, Ingredion appeared to be a credible and financially capable buyer with clear strategic reasons for acquiring the Company.

On August 12, 2014, Mr. Hatfield discussed the Ingredion indication of interest with Mr. Behrens to determine if SEACOR was interested in acquiring the Company. Mr. Behrens stated that SEACOR was not interested in participating in any potential sale process at that time.

On August 20 and 21, 2014, Ingredion and Penford negotiated provisions relating to, and exchanged drafts of, a nondisclosure and standstill agreement. Penford again declined to enter into exclusive negotiations with Ingredion. On August 21, 2014, Ingredion and Penford executed a nondisclosure and standstill agreement that superseded the prior Secrecy Agreement.

The board and Deutsche Bank also discussed the “market check” process whereby companies other than Ingredion would be queried by Deutsche Bank about their potential interest in acquiring the Company. Deutsche Bank again reviewed with the board the other potential counterparties and the basis for identifying such counterparties, including, but not limited to, financial capacity, perceived level of interest, prior acquisition track record, strong strategic rationale and ability to timely execute a transaction. Deutsche Bank further noted that any inquiries to potential counterparties likely would be directed to the chief executive officers of selected companies, with any substantive exchanges of information about the Company to be subject to an acceptable nondisclosure agreement. The board of directors discussed the due diligence and market check processes, including the companies that would be contacted in the market check process and any risks of loss of confidentiality during the process. Following discussion, the board directed management to continue negotiations with Ingredion and engage in the due diligence process with Ingredion. The board also authorized management and Deutsche Bank to proceed with the market check process as described at the meeting.

Throughout early- to mid-September, in accordance with the board’s authorization, Deutsche Bank contacted six potential strategic counterparties in the same or similar industries to gauge initial interest regarding a potential transaction with Penford.
On September 9, 2014, Party A expressed interest in having further discussions regarding a transaction and Deutsche Bank forwarded Party A a nondisclosure and standstill agreement.

Also on September 9, 2014, another strategic counterparty contacted by Deutsche Bank (referred to as Party B) expressed interest in further discussions, and Deutsche Bank forwarded a nondisclosure and standstill agreement to proceed with additional discussions with Party B.

On September 10, 2014, a third potential strategic counterparty contacted by Deutsche Bank (referred to as Party C) expressed interest in pursuing a transaction, and Deutsche Bank forwarded Party C a nondisclosure and standstill agreement on September 11, 2014.

On September 11, 2014, a fourth potential strategic counterparty contacted by Deutsche Bank (referred to as Party D) expressed interest, and Deutsche Bank forwarded a nondisclosure and standstill agreement to Party D.
On September 12, 2014, Party B informed Deutsche Bank that it had decided not to move forward with discussions or sign a nondisclosure agreement with respect to a potential transaction.

Also on September 12, 2014, Deutsche Bank left a voicemail for another strategic counterparty (referred to as Party E) regarding a potential transaction. In the following two weeks, Deutsche Bank also left several other voicemails for Party E regarding a potential transaction and received no response.

On September 15, 2014, Penford and Party C executed a nondisclosure and standstill agreement, which included standard confidentiality restrictions and a standstill provision that automatically expired upon the public announcement of the entering into a definitive agreement for a sale of 50% or more of the capital stock of Penford, and Penford provided Party C with information regarding Penford including, but not limited to, information regarding Penford’s corporate strategy, business segments, product applications and technologies, end markets, supply chain and manufacturing facilities, historical financial performance and financial projections.

On September 17, 2014, Mr. Fortnum contacted Mr. Malkoski to update him on Ingredion’s board meeting that was held on September 16, 2014, reporting that Ingredion’s board had discussed Ingredion’s strategy, including the proposed acquisition of Penford. Mr. Fortnum indicated during the conversation that Ingredion could potentially increase its proposal from $18.00 to $18.25 or $18.50 per share on a fully diluted basis.

On September 23, 2014, Penford and Party D executed a nondisclosure and standstill agreement, which included standard confidentiality restrictions and a standstill provision that automatically expired upon the public announcement of the entering into a definitive agreement for a sale of 50% or more of the capital stock of Penford.
The board determined that Party F should be approached regarding a potential transaction with Penford, but due to the factors noted above, Party G should not be contacted.
On September 24, 2014, in accordance with the board’s authorization, Deutsche Bank contacted Party F regarding a potential transaction with Penford.
On September 29, 2014, Party F communicated to Deutsche Bank that a combination with Penford was not a suitable strategic fit and declined to pursue further discussions regarding a transaction.

On September 30, 2014, Penford and Party A executed a nondisclosure and standstill agreement, which included standard confidentiality restrictions and a standstill provision that automatically expired upon the public announcement of the entering into a definitive agreement for a sale of 50% or more of the capital stock of Penford, and Deutsche Bank provided Party A with information regarding Penford’s operations, business segments, product applications and technologies, end markets, key leadership, supply chain and manufacturing facilities, historical financial performance and financial projections.

On October 2, 2014, Penford received a letter from Ingredion regarding its indication of interest and increasing the proposed price to $19.00 per share of outstanding Penford capital stock on a fully diluted basis.

Also on October 8, 2014, Deutsche Bank met with representatives from Party D, who indicated that it did not intend to move forward with discussions regarding a potential transaction involving the Company.

Later on October 14, 2014, Party A provided a formal letter with its indication of interest with respect to an acquisition of the Company at a price of $16.00 per share, citing increased volatility in the markets as the reason for the decrease in the offer price.
During the evening on October 14, 2014, Penford and Ingredion finalized and executed the merger agreement, and Penford, Ingredion and SEACOR executed the voting and support agreement.
Penford and Ingredion issued press releases announcing the transaction on October 15, 2014 prior to the opening of trading.

cohorts.label: Six strategic counterparties contacted in the market check
cohorts.membership_basis: Potential strategic counterparties in the same or similar industries contacted by Deutsche Bank in early- to mid-September 2014 to gauge initial interest regarding a potential transaction with Penford.

observations.summary: SEACOR filed a Schedule 13D amendment stating its intent to nominate four candidates for election to Penford’s board at the next annual meeting.
Ingredion advised Penford of its interest in acquiring Penford.
Penford and Ingredion executed a Secrecy Agreement to facilitate a limited exchange of confidential information.
The Executive Committee directed management to retain Deutsche Bank as the Company’s financial advisor and to negotiate an engagement letter.
Ingredion orally indicated it was prepared to submit a proposal to acquire the Company and suggested a price of $17.00 per share on a fully diluted basis.
Ingredion sent Penford a letter confirming its interest in a possible business combination and providing an indicative valuation of $18.00 per share in cash.
SEACOR stated that it was not interested in participating in any potential sale process at that time.
Ingredion and Penford executed a nondisclosure and standstill agreement that superseded the prior Secrecy Agreement.
The board directed management to continue negotiations and due diligence with Ingredion and authorized management and Deutsche Bank to proceed with the market check process.
Deutsche Bank contacted six potential strategic counterparties in the same or similar industries to gauge initial interest regarding a potential transaction with Penford.
observations.other_detail: Initial outreach to gauge interest regarding a potential transaction with Penford.

Ingredion indicated that it could potentially increase its proposal to $18.25 or $18.50 per share on a fully diluted basis.

observations.summary: Penford and Party D executed a nondisclosure and standstill agreement.
The board determined that Party G should not be contacted regarding a potential transaction with Penford.
Deutsche Bank contacted Party F regarding a potential transaction with Penford.

observations.summary: Party F communicated that a combination with Penford was not a suitable strategic fit and declined to pursue further discussions regarding a transaction.
Penford and Party A executed a nondisclosure and standstill agreement, and Deutsche Bank provided Party A with information regarding Penford.
Penford received a letter from Ingredion increasing the proposed price to $19.00 per share on a fully diluted basis.
Party D indicated that it did not intend to move forward with discussions regarding a potential transaction involving the Company.
Party A provided a formal letter with an indication of interest to acquire the Company at $16.00 per share.
Penford and Ingredion finalized and executed the merger agreement.
Penford, Ingredion and SEACOR executed the voting and support agreement.
Penford and Ingredion executed the merger agreement.

exclusions.explanation: B005 describes unsolicited indications of interest and confidentiality agreements in 2007 and 2009 from two unnamed industry parties. It is historical background outside the 2014 sale process and does not support current-window party or observation rows.

Party B was forwarded a nondisclosure and standstill agreement on September 9, 2014, but on September 12, 2014 it decided not to move forward or sign the agreement, so no NDA agreement row is emitted.
B050 reports repeated voicemails to Party E and no response. The outreach is covered by the market-check solicitation, but no separate affirmative Party E observation is emitted.
